name: Attic truss layout
description: Top-floor layout tucked directly under a pitched roof, where the rafter geometry rather than the walls dictates how the space can be inhabited. The sloped ceilings cut steeply down on both long sides, so usable floor area collapses to a central spine running parallel to the ridge — typically three to four blocks wide — where occupants can stand at full height. The flanking eaves zones still receive floor area but with progressively lower headroom, making them ideal for low furniture: beds, chests, knee-wall storage, built-in benches. The layout suits a single bedroom, a children's loft, bulk storage, or a small studio under the eaves. Dormers punched through the slope add localised pockets of full headroom and the only practical way to fit a window without breaking the roofline.
placement_rules: Align the long axis of the attic with the ridge of the roof above so the central full-height spine runs the full length of the floor; cross-axial attics waste headroom against gables.
Reserve the central 3-4 block-wide spine as standing-height circulation and tall furniture zone; everything within one block of the eaves should be treated as low-headroom storage only.
Place beds, low chests and built-in benches against the knee walls under the slope where headroom is 1-2 blocks; do not place doors, wardrobes or any furniture taller than 2 blocks in this zone.

Punch dormers through the slope wherever a window or a workspace needs full headroom; one dormer per major function (bed nook, desk, reading chair) is typical, capped at three on a single attic to avoid roof structural conflicts.
Locate the attic access — ladder hatch or quarter-turn attic stair — at one end of the spine, never under the lowest part of the slope, so a person ascending lands with full headroom.
Keep gable ends as the primary location for full-height windows and exterior doors; the gable walls are the only vertical surfaces tall enough to host a normal opening without a dormer.
lighting_ventilation: Daylight comes from two complementary sources: gable-end windows light the spine along its long axis, and dormer windows punched through the slope light the eaves zones locally where occupants actually sit or sleep. Lanterns hung from the ridge beam supplement at night and emphasise the linear geometry of the spine. Cross-ventilation works naturally between opposing gables; if dormers are added on both slopes they double as ventilation outlets.

rustic.notes: Exposed oak log rafters and plank ceilings; the truss geometry is left visible rather than concealed, and dormers are framed in stripped spruce logs to match the timber feel.

medieval.notes: Heavy dark oak king-post trusses over cobblestone knee walls; dormers are small and steep-roofed, evoking a guildhall loft or castle servant quarters.

victorian.notes: Plastered ceilings between dark beams, gable-end sash windows and ornamental brick-trimmed dormers; the attic reads as a finished servant's room or nursery rather than raw storage.
